coredata.dc:title: Improved lithium storage capacity and high rate capability of nitrogen-doped graphite-like electrode materials prepared from thermal pyrolysis of graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Gu, Siyong
Christensen, Tommiejean
Hsieh, Chien-Te
Mallick, Bikash Chandra
Gandomi, Yasser Ashraf
Li, Jianlin
Chang, Jeng-Kuei
coredata.dc:description: Adopting a solid-phase microwave-assisted technique followed by thermal pyrolysis of N-functionalized graphene quantum dots, novel nitrogen-doped graphite-like (NGL) electrode materials were synthesized and served as the anode for Li-ion batteries. The NGL anode demonstrated reversible capacity of 530 mAh g−1 at 0.1C, superior rate capability at high C rate operation (420 mAh g−1 at 5C), remarkable initial coulombic efficiency (>95.7%), and excellent cyclic stability along with high efficiency (>99.1%) during entire cycling. The NGL anode nanostructure enables improved lithium ion mobility and reversible Li+ storage during cycling. The analysis of the Ragone plots revealed that the specific energy of NGL anode reaches to ca. 840 Wh kg−1 at the power density of 4200 W kg−1. The diffusion coefficient of Li ions was measured as 1.69 × 10−9 cm2 s−1 for the NGL anode material, substantially improving over commonly used graphite electrodes (15–26 times higher Li+ diffusivity). The high-rate cyclability as well as the cyclic stability of the NGL anodes were also confirmed via long-term cycling of full pouch cells assembled with ternary cathode and NGL anode. The robust design of the NGL anode materials introduced in this work, paves the way for designing next-generation lithium-ion batteries operating at ultra-high C rates.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen doping
Graphite-like anodes
Lithium ion battery
Graphene quantum dots
Rate capability

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Program for Innovative Research Team in Science and Technology in Fujian Province University

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are very grateful for the financial support from the Ministry of Science and Technology Taiwan ( MOST 108-2221-E-155-036-MY3) . Partial financial support for this work under the grant number CMRPD2E0082 by the Chang Gung Memorial Hospital, Linkou, Taiwan (Chang Gung Medical Foundation, Taiwan) is deeply appreciated. Prof. Gu also thanks for the support from the Talents Introduction Program of Xiamen University of Technology ( YKJ19018R ) and Program for Innovative Research Team in Science and Technology in Fujian Province University (IRTSTFJ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FE-SEM images of different NGL samples: (a) C1, (b) C2, and (c) C3. HR-TEM micrographs of different NGL samples: (d) C1, (e) C2, and (f) C3. The inset of the HR-TEM micrograph shows their corresponding SAD pattern.
(a) Typical XRD patterns and (b) Raman spectra of different NGL samples.
XPS C 1s and N 1s peaks of different NGL samples: (a,d) C1, (b,e) C2, and (c,f) C3. Herein all XPS peaks were deconvoluted by a multiple Gaussian function.

(a) Typical charge-discharge curves of traditional graphite and NGL anodes at 0.1C, (b) specific capacity and coulombic efficiency as a function of cycle number at different rates, (c) capacity retention as a function of C rate, and (d) cyclic stability of different NGL anodes at 1C, where the inset shows one photograph of light-emitting-diode device powered by the NGL cell.
The schematic of Li storage mechanism in three-dimensional NGL crystals, possessing more active sites for Li storage and more pore channels for Li migration.
Ragone plots of coin cells equipped with NGL anodes.

(a) Nyquist plots of different anodes at OCV, where an equivalent circuit was proposed for describing the EIS behavior within the frequency range between 100 kHz and 1 mHz. (b) Randles plots of different NGL anodes at OCV.

(a) Discharge capacity of full cell (NCM//C3) as a function of cycle number at different rates, and (b) cyclic stability of the cell at 0.1C, where the photographs show the C3 anode sheet and the pouch cell.
Surface characteristics of N-doped graphite-like samples determined by nitrogen physisorption method at −196 °C.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para:
S
BET: Specific surface area computed using BET equation.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: S t: Specific surface area determined by Langmuir adsorption method.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: V t: Total pore volume estimated at a relative pressure of 0.98.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: d
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: X micro: Micropore fraction determined from the subtraction of mesopore fraction from total pore volume fraction.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: e
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: X meso: Mesopore fraction determined from the ratio of Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) mesopore volume to total pore volume.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Components and related parameters of the equivalent circuit for N-doped graphite-like electrodes. Diffusion coefficients of Li+ ions in the electrodes determined by Fig. 7(b).

head.ce:title: Improved lithium storage capacity and high rate capability of nitrogen-doped graphite-like electrode materials prepared from thermal pyrolysis of graphene quantum dots

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical Engineering and Materials Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Adopting a solid-phase microwave-assisted technique followed by thermal pyrolysis of N-functionalized graphene quantum dots, novel nitrogen-doped graphite-like (NGL) electrode materials were synthesized and served as the anode for Li-ion batteries. The NGL anode demonstrated reversible capacity of 530 mAh g−1 at 0.1C, superior rate capability at high C rate operation (420 mAh g−1 at 5C), remarkable initial coulombic efficiency (>95.7%), and excellent cyclic stability along with high efficiency (>99.1%) during entire cycling. The NGL anode nanostructure enables improved lithium ion mobility and reversible Li+ storage during cycling. The analysis of the Ragone plots revealed that the specific energy of NGL anode reaches to ca. 840 Wh kg−1 at the power density of 4200 W kg−1. The diffusion coefficient of Li ions was measured as 1.69 × 10−9 cm2 s−1 for the NGL anode material, substantially improving over commonly used graphite electrodes (15–26 times higher Li+ diffusivity). The high-rate cyclability as well as the cyclic stability of the NGL anodes were also confirmed via long-term cycling of full pouch cells assembled with ternary cathode and NGL anode. The robust design of the NGL anode materials introduced in this work, paves the way for designing next-generation lithium-ion batteries operating at ultra-high C rates.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) consist of two electrodes (i.e. anode and cathode), a separator, and electrolyte [1] where typical anode and cathodes are graphite and lithium metal oxide (e.g. lithium cobalt oxide, lithium manganese oxide, and lithium iron phosphate), respectively. Despite having a flat discharge curve within a wide state of charge (SoC) window and superior stability, graphite has a relatively low specific theoretical capacity (i.e., 372 mAh g−1). Also, LIBs utilizing a graphite anode usually demonstrate a poor rate capability and cannot be cycled at high rates [2–4]. Therefore, alternative anode materials must be developed for boasting the specific energy and power density as well as the rate capability for next-generation LIBs. Several prior studies have reported on the application of the metal oxides (e.g., SnO2 [5], Co3O4 [6], ZnO [7]) and silicon [8] as promising anode materials for LIBs. However, a severe volume expansion (approximately 200–300 vol%) as well as rapid crack propagation within the metal oxide anode has been observed during cycling [8–10]. Similar undesired deformation has been reported for Si-based anodes [8,10]. Therefore, maintaining a stable solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) layer has not yet been achieved using the metal oxides or Si-based anode materials [9].

Another type of anode material is graphene which has also been widely investigated for LIBs [11,12]. However, poor cycling performance at high charge-discharge rates as well as reduced coulombic efficiency within the initial stages of cycling has been identified as the major drawbacks. The SEI layer formed on the surface of the pure graphene anode during cycling usually leads to electrolyte decomposition, and subsequently results in an irreversible capacity decay. The undesired SEI layer developed on the graphene anode hinders the efficacy of the charge transport across the electrode/electrolyte interface [13–15]. Also, the remaining oxygen functionalities (e.g. carbonyl, hydroxyl and carboxyl groups) on the reduced graphene promote electrolyte decomposition and consequently decrease the stability of the graphene anode [16].

To overcome the issues associated with the SEI layer formation on the graphene anode as well as the crack propagation within the metal oxide or Si-based materials, various hybrid anode designs (e.g. Si/graphene or metal oxide/graphene composites) have been explored. Although performance enhancement has been reported utilizing the composite anode materials, further improvements in energy density, high-rate capability, and long-term cyclability is needed. One promising approach towards achieving these objectives is to design advanced materials with finely tuned nanostructure.

Recent progress in doping graphene with heteroatoms such as electron-rich nitrogen has enabled in-plane substitution of N atoms, making the carbon material an n-type semiconductor [17,18]. Efforts have been made to synthesize N-doped carbon materials using refluxing in ammonium hydroxide [19], hydrothermal synthesis in presence of dopamine [20], carbonization of graphitic-C3N4@phenolic resin [21], pyrolysis in presence of NH4Cl, carbonization of the mixture of polyacrylonitrile/N,N-dimethylformamide/graphene oxide [22], hydrothermal and annealing in presence of urea [23], and thermal treatment in NH3/Ar atmosphere [24]. By introducing nitrogen to defect-containing carbonaceous structures, not only the nitrogen content in carbon structures affects the electrocatalytic activity but also several parameters such as porosity and electrochemical surface area [19]. Recently, we demonstrated a solid-phase microwave-assisted (SPMA) technique for synthesizing N-doped graphene quantum dots (GQDs) using low-cost citric acid + urea solid mixture as the precursor [25]. The facile SPMA method enables synthesizing N-/O-functionalized GQDs with high production yield (ca. 40–50 wt%) at the pilot scale; thus, very promising for large-scale commercialization. Nitrogen doping into carbon lattice have been confirmed to significantly improve the electrochemical properties of carbonaceous host materials due to an enhanced electronic structure [26,27]. Theoretical studies also revealed that doping graphene with N is an effective approach for tailoring the electronic property as well as chemical reactivity due to stronger electronegativity of nitrogen (3.5) compared to that of carbon (3.0) from one side and conjugation between the nitrogen lone pair electrons and the graphite π-system from the other side [28–30]. This prior finding inspired us to develop a novel technique capable of thermally stripping the oxygen functional groups from the GQD samples as well as chemically doping nitrogen into the carbon network.

The thermal pyrolysis of N-doped GQDs was performed at 1000 °C under high-purity hydrogen atmosphere, forming N-doped graphite-like (NGL) materials. The NGL samples were fabricated in the form of half-coin cells and pouch cells equipped with LiNi0.5Co0.2Mn0.3O2 (NCM) cathodes, and the cells were characterized in-situ via charge-discharge cycling at different C rates (i.e. 0.1–5C) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy. The influence of N doping on the electrochemical performance of the anode has also been systematically investigated. We have shown that the resulting NGL samples exhibit superior yet stable performance as the anode material for LIBs. Since the precursors (i.e. citric acid and urea) is inexpensive and the SPMA method is facile and efficient (high yield/high production rate), we believe that the NGL anode paves the way for designing next-generation lithium-ion batteries capable of efficiently operating at higher C rates (i.e. fast charge and discharge capability). The merit of the present work is to develop an efficient method for preparing N-doped graphite-like anode materials for high-rate and high-energy Li-ion batteries.

In our previous study, we have elaborated on the SPMA technique for growing N-functionalized GQDs under microwave irradiation [25]. In short, the GQD precursor was initially prepared through a mixture of citric acid, urea and distilled water; subsequently, the solution was heated in a homemade microwave reactor (nominal power: 6000 W) at 280 °C for 5 min. Three samples (ca. 100 g) with the citric acid to urea weight ratio of 2/1 (labeled as C1), 1/1 (C2), and 1/2 (C3) were prepared and placed within the reactor at rotational speed of 60 rpm. The resulting samples were analyzed using an inductively-coupled plasma optical emission spectroscopy/mass spectrometry (ICP-OES/MS) and a high N/C atomic ratio was achieved (34, 48, and 66 at.% for C1, C2, and C3 GQD samples, respectively). The O/C atomic ratios was also quantified with the ICP-OES/MS analysis, and a high oxidation level was confirmed (56–61 at.%), as shown in the
Electronic
Supporting
Information
(see Table S1). High-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) micrographs of N-doped GQD samples were given in Figure S1. In the next step, the resulting GQD samples were placed inside a high-temperature furnace and then thermally treated at 1000 °C under pure H2 atmosphere. The thermal pyrolysis process was carried out for 1 h, and the treated NGL samples were cooled down to the ambient temperature.

The morphology and structure of the NGL samples were characterized by the field-emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, JEOL JSM-5600) and HR-TEM (FEI Talos F200s). The HR-TEM microscopy was conducted using a microscope operating at 200 kV. To prepare the NGL samples, the hybrid powder was ultrasonically dispersed in ethanol, and 0.05 mL (∼a drop) of the NGL suspension was applied onto a copper grid and was subsequently dried in air. An X-ray diffraction (XRD, Shimadzu Labx XRD-6000) spectroscope, equipped with Cu-Kα radiation emitter, was employed for analyzing the crystalline structures of the samples. The crystallization degree of the NGL samples was analyzed using Raman spectroscopy (Renishaw Micro-Raman spectrometer). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Fison VG ESCA210) with a Mg-Kα emitter, was used to characterize the chemical composition of the samples. The resulting C 1s, N 1s and O 1s spectra were deconvoluted implementing a non-linear least square fitting algorithm with a symmetric Gaussian function. The surface characteristics of the samples were assessed via the gas adsorption technique. An automated adsorption apparatus (Micromeritics, ASAP 2020) was employed and the specific surface area along with the mesopore volume of the samples were determined from the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) and Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) equations, respectively.

The NGL samples (i.e., C1, C2, and C3) were homogeneously mixed with a binder (polyvinylidenefluoride (PVdF)) and two conducting media (Super-P and KS-6) at the ratio of 93.8 : 5.0: 1.0 : 0.2 wt (i.e., NGL: PVdF: Super P: KS-6) in N-methyl pyrrolidinone (NMP) solvent to form the anode slurries. The mixture was then blended in a three-dimensional mixer using Zr balls for 3 h to form uniform slurry. The resulting slurry was uniformly pasted on a Cu foil substrate with a doctor blade, followed by evaporating the solvent, NMP, with a blow dryer. The as-prepared anode sheets were dried at 85 °C in a vacuum oven for 12 h and subsequently were pressed at 200 kg cm−2. The electrode layers were then adjusted to have a thickness of ca. 100 μm with a mass loading of 15 mg cm−2. The CR2032-type coin cells were assembled in a glove box for the electrochemical characterization. In the coin cell assembly, the Li foil and porous polypropylene film served as the counter electrode and separator, respectively. The electrolyte solution included a lithium salt (1.0 M LiPF6) dissolved in a mixture of ethylene carbonate, propylene carbonate, and dimethyl carbonate with a weight ratio of 1:1:1. The half cells were charged implementing a commonly used constant current-constant voltage protocol (i.e. different C rates to 0.01 V with a 0.017 mA cut-off current). The cycling test at different C rates (0.1–5C) was performed within the voltage range of 0.01–1.6 V vs. Li/Li+ at ambient temperature. For comparison, the artificial graphite with a particle size of 10–20 μm (Ubiq Technology Co., Taoyuan, Taiwan) was employed as an anode material to provide the baseline operating conditions. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS, CH Instruments 608C) was employed to assess the electrochemical performance of as-prepared Li-ion batteries. The EIS measurement was carried out at open-circuit voltage within the frequency range of 100 k‒0.01 Hz.

To provide more in-depth analysis, further experimental tests were conducted with pouch cells assembled with commercial NCM and NGL as the cathode and anode materials, respectively. Herein the NCM powder was provided from Union Chemical Ind. Co. (Taiwan). The NCM cathode consisted of NCM (94.8 wt%), PVdF (4 wt%), Super-P (1 wt%), and KS-6 (0.2 wt%). The cathode slurry was pasted onto an Al foil, following a similar coating procedure conducted for the assembly of NGL anode. Both NCM and NGL electrode sheets were designed to have different mass loading, ca. 36 and 15 mg cm−2, respectively. The NCM cathode sheets were subsequently cut into 111.5 × 45 mm2, whereas the area of NGL anode sheets was ca. 112 × 46 mm2. The discharge capacity of NCM cathode was approximately 170 mAh g−1 at 0.1C. The capacity ratio of anode to cathode was set ca. 1.2–1.3 for the design of pouch cells. The electrical conductivity of the electrode sheets was measured based on the four-probe technique elaborated elsewhere. One thin-film probing system (KeithLink Technology Co., Ltd.) was employed to analyze the electrical conductivity (σ avg) of as-prepared electrode sheets. The probe diameter and the probe spacing distance were ca. 100 μm and 1 μm, respectively. The σ avg values were then determined through averaging five different measurements recorded at various points throughout the electrode sheets. The charge/discharge cycling experiments were carried out at different C rates (varied from 0.5 to 5C) within the voltage range of 2.75–4.25 V at ambient temperature.

The nanostructured morphology of as-prepared NGL samples was investigated using FE-SEM and HR-TEM spectroscopy, as shown in Fig. 1 . As illustrated in Fig. 1(a)-1(c), both C1 and C2 samples include an interconnected porous flake-like structure, while C3 sample exhibits a porous and disordered network consisting of thin layers of stacked nanosheets. According to Fig. 1, the nanostructure of the samples is vastly different from the morphology of N-functionalized GO prior to the thermal pyrolysis treatment [25]. HR-TEM micrographs, as depicted in Fig. 1(d)-1(f), further exhibit the flake-like structure of C1 and C2 frameworks, while C3 sample is composed of curly sheets, forming a relatively porous cluster. The corresponding selected-area diffraction pattern, located at the inset of Fig. 1(d)-1(f)), illustrates several ring-like structures, demonstrating typical features of the hexagonal graphite lattices [31]. However, the incomplete rings of the NGL samples reveal the disordered structure of carbon lattices due to the presence of structural defects caused by the nitrogen doping [32].

Typical N2 adsorption and desorption isotherms for the NGL samples, synthesized via thermal pyrolysis of N-functionalized GQDs, are provided in Figure S2. All the NGL samples exhibit a capillary condensation region and a distinct hysteresis characteristic at high pressure domain within the adsorption isotherms, (i.e. typical Type II isotherm) [33]. This reveals that a mesoporous nanostructure has been formed. To analyze the surface characteristics of NGL samples, the BET surface area and BJH mesopore volume were employed and the N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms were assessed (see Table 1 ). As tabulated in Table 1, the as-prepared NGL electrodes have BET surface area of 6.13, 8.83, and 11.7 m2 g−1 for C1, C2, and C3 samples, respectively. Therefore, the BET surface area measured for the samples demonstrate an increasing trend with the oxygen and nitrogen content within N-functionalized GQDs. Herein, we also observe that a significant portion of the total pore volume consist of mesoporous structure. The formation of the mesoporous internal structure is due to the oxygen/nitrogen functionalities imposing a large number of broken bonds, defects, cavities, and N-doping at high temperature, as confirmed by the FE-SEM and HR-TEM images (see Fig. 1). Among the NGL samples, C3 sample possesses highest BET surface area, the largest total volume, and the highest mesopore fraction. The novel structure of the porous network observed for sample C3 is contributed to higher accessible surface area providing an efficient diffusion pathway for the Li ions during charge/discharge cycling.

Fig. 2 (a) shows typical XRD patterns of different NGL samples, demonstrating a weak and broad (002) and (100) diffraction peaks at ca. 25.2–25.8° and 43.2–43.5°, respectively. In contrast, the artificial graphite possesses well-indexed diffraction peaks with ultra-high intensity, as depicted in Figure S3. The interlayer distance, d (002), of graphitic crystalline can be obtained using the Bragg’s equation [34]. According to Figure S3, the (002) peak of the artificial graphite occurs at 26.4° (d (002) = 0.337 nm) whereas for the NGL samples the peak appears at 2θ = 25.2–25.8° (d (002) = 0.345–0.351 nm) indicating a larger interlayer spacing within the NGL crystals. The larger interlayer spacing indicates the presence of nitrogen doping in the NGL samples. The other crystalline parameter of carbonaceous materials, the mean crystalline size along the c-axis, L C, can also be calculated from the peak width evaluated at the coordinate associated with the half-maximal d (002) peak, using Debye-Scherrer’s equation. Employing the Debye-Scherrer’s equation, L C for the artificial graphite was 24.6 nm, whereas the L C values of NGL samples fall within the range of 2.10–2.15 nm. The mean number of graphitic wall (N) can be computed by using the ratio of L C to d (002) value [35]. The calculation regarding the crystalline parameters of NGL samples was given in Appendix 1 [36,37]. As expected, the N values of NGL samples are close to 6, which is much lower than that of artificial graphite (∼73 layers). As compared to those of artificial graphite, such small magnitudes of L C and N values of NGL samples, mainly originated from the removal of oxygen (e.g. carboxylic group) and nitrogen (e.g. amino group) functionalities from N-functionalized GQDs during the thermal reduction in H2 atmosphere. It is important to note that the removal of C atoms have also been occurred along with the O-/N-containing functional groups during the thermal reduction process. Thus, the discontinuous graphene defects emerged within the NGL samples lead to reduced crystal sizes.

Typical Raman spectra of as-prepared NGL samples are illustrated in Fig. 2(b), demonstrating two broad peaks appearing at ca. 1580 and ca. 1350 cm−1, associated with the well-defined G and D bands, respectively. Generally, the G band is the characteristic of the first order scattering of the E2g mode and indicates the formation of graphitic carbon. The D band corresponds to the mode of the sp2-rings of the disordered graphene layer induced by various defects (such as hybridization caused by heteroatom doping and structure defects by thermal treatment) [26,38]. The ratio of the D to G peak intensities (I D/I G) is an important index demonstrating the graphitization degree of the carbon-based materials. The I D/I G ratio of C1, C2, and C3 samples were 0.93, 0.94, and 0.96, respectively, much higher than that of the artificial graphite (∼0.10). The substantial increase of the I D/I G ratio for the NGL samples is due to the defects formed due to N doping onto the carbon framework [39]. Thus, achieving a higher pyrrolic/pyridinic N-doping level results in an increased graphitization degree within the carbon nanostructure.

To analyze the elemental composition as well as the nitrogen bonding configuration of the NGL samples the XPS was conducted. Figures S4 shows survey-scan XPS and O 1s peaks of different NGL samples, deconvoluted by the multiple Gaussian function. All NGL samples show three remarkable peaks at ca. 284.5, 531.5, and 400.0 eV corresponding to C 1s, O 1s, and N 1s spectra, respectively [26,40]. The N/C atomic ratios of C1, C2, and C3 samples dramatically decreased to 3.8, 4.7, and 4.2 at.% upon the thermal pyrolysis in hydrogen. Similarly, the O/C ratios were reduced to 3.6, 3.8, and 2.1 for C1, C2, and C3 samples, respectively (see Table S1). This result reflects that the thermal pyrolysis is capable of doping N atoms into the NGL samples as well as removing significant portion of the oxygen-containing functional groups. Reduced O/C ratio on the NGL samples hinders the SEI formation within the initial stages of cycling and subsequently reduces the irreversible capacity decay of the graphite anodes [41]. Based on the analysis of the high-resolution O 1s spectra of the NGL samples (see Figure S4), three different O components can be identified at ∼531.1, 532.3, and 533.6 eV, corresponding to CO, C–OH, and HO–CO, respectively [26]. It is worth noting that both C1 and C2 samples contain one additional component at ca. 536.1 eV, associated with the chemisorbed water molecules.

As shown in Fig. 3 (a)-(c), the C 1s spectra of NGL samples can be resolved into four peaks with different binding energies of 284.5, 285.8, 287.5, and 289.5 eV, corresponding to sp3 C–C/sp2 CC bonds of graphitic carbon, hydroxyl group (C–OH), carbonyl group (CO), and carboxyl group (HO–CO), respectively [42]. Since most oxygen functionalities were stripped from the GQD surface during the thermal pyrolysis, the C 1s peaks can be deconvoluted into two components at around 285.8 and 287.5 eV, assigned to sp2 C and sp3 C atoms bonded to N atoms, respectively [26,43]. This result reveals that (i) the substitution of N atoms takes place at the edge or defective sites of the NGL samples or (ii) N atoms are successfully incorporated into the graphite lattice. To further support this finding, the N 1s peaks were decomposed into three components at ca. 398.3, 400.6 and 401.5 eV, related to pyridinic, pyrrolic, and quaternary N (or graphitic N), respectively [28,43]. As depicted in Fig. 3(d)-3(f), we observe that all NGL samples contain pyridinic, pyrrolic, and quaternary N; however, the N-group distributions vary among the NGL samples. The sum of pyridinic + pyrrolic N on the NGL samples had the following order: C3 (83.4%) > C1 (77.4%) > C2 (72.2%). Theoretically, pyridinic N bonds with two C atoms with one p-electron and pyrrolic N with two p-electrons localized in the π conjugated system, whereas graphitic N substitutes a C atom in the hexagonal ring [28]. Thus, theoretical calculations on N-doped carbons suggest that pyridinic and pyrrolic N was p-type doping, while substitution of C atoms with graphitic N was n-type doping [44–46]; thus, the N-doped electrodes demonstrate higher electrical conductivity due to enhanced carrier mobility.

Fig. 4 (a) displays the presentative charge-discharge profiles of artificial graphite (denoted as G) and NGL electrodes within the potential range of 0.01–1.6 V vs. Li/Li+ at 0.1C. As illustrated in Fig. 4(a), the G electrode demonstrates a symmetric profile during charge-discharge process, exhibiting very flat plateau below 0.2 V, corresponding to the lithiation/delithiation between crystalline graphite and Li ions. As for NGL electrodes, there is no typical lithium insertion/extraction plateau at low potentials due to disordered stacking of the N-doped graphite-like structures, resulting in electrochemically and geometrically nonequivalent Li ion sites [47–49]. The specific capacities of G, C1, C2, and C3 electrodes were 338, 382, 425, 530 mAh g−1 at 0.1C, respectively. According to Fig. 4, the capacity of C3 electrode was increased up to 56.8%, as compared to the G electrode; thus, a larger reversible Li-storage capacity was achieved through the utilization of the NGL electrodes. The variation of specific capacity and coulombic efficiency with cycle number at various C rates is depicted in Fig. 4(b). The initial coulombic efficiency of all NGL electrodes was measured within the range of 94.6–95.7%. The high coulombic efficiency is ascribed to ultralow oxidation extent on the NGL electrodes, preventing the SEI layer from chemically bonding to the surface ‒COOLi and ‒OLi groups [41]. Based on the accessible capacity of the graphite anodes, the C rates, 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1, 2 and 5C, correspond to the current densities of ca. 35, 70, 175, 350, 700, and 1750 mA g−1, respectively. As shown in Fig. 4(b), the NGL electrodes outperform the G electrode at various cycling conditions. In particular, substantial improvement in the performance and rate capability at high C rate operation (i.e. 5C, fast charging) was achieved utilizing the NGL electrodes. According to Fig. 4(b), employing the C3 electrode, an ultra-high specific capacity (i.e. 420 mAh g−1) was successfully demonstrated at 5C. A table for comparison among N-doped carbon materials for Li-ion batteries is given in Table 2 [50–53]. We observe that the N-doped carbon anodes exhibit an improved capacity ranged from 419 to 1518 mAh g−1 at low rates. This achievement also confirms the positive effect on the improved capacity due to nitrogen doping into carbon-based anodes, as compared to conventional graphite anode (theoretical capacity: 372 mAh g−1). This reveals that N-dopants (pyridinic N and pyrrolic N) offer extra Li-storage sites in the N-doped carbon lattices [50]. It is worth noting that the maximal capacities of N-doped carbon [50], N-doped 3D carbon [52], N-doped hard carbon anodes [53] reach as high as 526, 1518, and 673 mAh g−1, approximate to or higher than that of NGL (C3) anode at 0.1C. However, the initial coulombic efficiency of the N-doped carbon electrodes ranged from 58.5 to 63.1%. The initial irreversible capacity loss mainly originates from the SEI layer forming, the electrolyte decomposition, and some irreversible reactions between the electrolyte and Li species [53]. This can be inferred from the presence of oxygen functionalities [51] and structural defects (due to nanosize and porosity) [52] that induce thicker SEI layers (e.g. Li2O and Li2CO3), causing severe Li-consumption and capacity degradation during the first cycles. The high oxidation level on the N-doped carbons raises not only irreversible capacity but also electrical conductivity of the anodes. As to C3 anode, thermal reduction in H2 is capable of reducing the O/C ratio (∼2.1 at.%) and repairing the disorder carbons in the NGL lattices, ensuring excellent Li-reversibility at the first cycle (i.e. 95.7%).

To probe the rate capability, the capacity retention was assessed at various C rates (i.e. 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1, 2 and 5C). As shown in Fig. 4(c), the capacity retention for C1, C2, C3, and G electrodes was 71.5, 82.4, 79.2, and 19.8%, respectively for the 5C cycling conditions. Therefore, the NGL electrodes enable an ultra-fast and reversible Li+ intercalation within the host structure. To explore the cyclic performance, the NGL electrodes were cyclically charged and discharged at 1C. The specific capacity and coulombic efficiency as a function of cycle number is depicted in Fig. 4(d). As illustrated in Fig. 4(d), all NGL electrodes exhibit a stable capacity for 200 cycles with a superb coulombic efficiency (i.e. > 98.0%).

Upon completion of 200 cycles, the coin cell equipped with C3 anode was used to power a white light-emitting-diode (LED), as shown in the inset of Fig. 4(d). The LED device was able to illuminate white light, confirming superior durability of the NGL anode material. Therefore, the NGL anode provides high Li-storage capacity, superior rate capability, and excellent cyclic stability, compared to commonly used graphite anode material. Among the NGL electrodes, the C3 sample, having highest urea to citric acid weight ratio, demonstrates remarkable specific capacity as well as capacity retention at ultra-high charge/discharge rates, confirming the robust design of NGL electrode materials for high performance Li-ion batteries. Table 2 also collects the information regarding the rate capability and cyclic performance of N-doped carbon anodes. It can be seen that the rate capability of N-doped carbon anodes was significantly improved by doping N elements into the carbon anodes. Although each N-doped anodes possess their own porous/lattice structure and electrode design (such as recipe of electrode slurry), the NGL electrode exhibit satisfactory rate capability and cyclic stability as compared to other N-doped carbon anodes [50–53]. The enhanced rate capability is ascribed to: (i) the doped N atoms intensify the reactivity and electrical conductivity of the NGL electrodes due to intrinsically higher electronegativity of N (3.04) compared to C (2.55), and consequently facilitate the electron transfer within the electrode nanostructure [51,53,54]; and (ii) doping N atoms into the electrode structure induces additional edge-structure (as shown in Figure S5) and provides extra flexible vacancy defects for enhanced Li-storage [26,50].

To further analyze the performance enhancement achieved through NGL anode materials, the active sites on the internal structure of the graphite during lithiation/delithiation process can be formulated as [6]:
(R1)
x Li+ + 6 C + x e− ↔ Li x C6

According to (R1), when Li ions are completely adsorbed on the graphite anode (i.e. x = 1) the LiC6 compound is formed with a theoretical capacity of 372 mAh g−1 [55]. As for NGL electrodes, the majority of N-5 defect site (i.e., pyrrolic N) bond with Li+ ions during the lithiation process, and the N-5 site can be refreshed upon delithiation [43], imparting additional sites for Li-storage and an extra capacity. Density-functional theory calculations have also predicted that two Li atoms can be intercalated in N-5 sites between 0 and 1.5 V, while N-6 defect sites (i.e., pyridinic N) enables extended capacity above 1.5 V [56]. Accordingly, the pyrrolic N site contributes to the improved capacity of the NGL anodes within the potential range of 0–1.5 V. According to the XPS N 1s analysis, C3 anode possesses the highest percentage of N-5 sites (∼66.7%) among the NGL samples; thus, providing further accessible sites for the Li storage. Accordingly, the Li-storage mechanism in the NGL anode can be expressed in Fig. 5 . First, Li ions are capable of freely migrating in three-dimensional NGL framework, reducing diffusion resistance. With the aid of N doping, the x values (see Eq. (R1)) were raised to 1.03, 1.14, and 1.42, forming the N-doped Li1.03C6, Li1.14C6, and Li1.42C6 compounds upon complete lithiation on C1, C2, and C3 anodes, respectively. This reveals that higher ratio of pyrrolic N significantly facilitates Li-storage capability due to high tendency of the Li ions to react with the N-doped carbon lattice. Due to higher electronegativity of N (3.5) compared to C (3.0) and relatively smaller atomic diameter, N atoms incorporated onto the carbon lattice promote the formation of the N-doped Li x C6 compounds with strong interactions between the NGL structure and the Li ions, favoring the Li insertion [26,57,58]. As illustrated in Fig. 4, the lithiation/delithiation process on the Li x C6 compounds is highly reversible with superior coulombic efficiency during the charge-discharge cycling.

It is well-established that for LIBs operating at high C rate, the specific energy density decreases substantially at high power density regimes. This is not desirable for future-generation EVs and HEVs demanding high-rate cyclability with smaller footprint [59]. To this end, we have analyzed the real-time performance of as-prepared electrodes. The specific energy and power density of the coin cells assembled with NGL anodes have been correlated in the form of a Ragone plot (see Fig. 6 ). The Ragone plots, as shown in Fig. 6, clearly demonstrate that all NGL anodes maintain superior Li-storage capacity even at high power density cycling. In contrast, the G anode exhibits a poor energy density at high power density operation, especially, a significant reduction in the energy density can be observed for operating at, or higher than 100 W kg−1. For C3 anode, the specific energy reaches to ca. 840 Wh kg−1 at the power density of 4200 W kg−1. Such exceptional performance makes NGL materials an ideal anode for high-performance LIBs.

The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was employed to analyze the impedance behavior of the LIBs with NGL anodes. Fig. 7 (a) shows Nyquist plots of the electrodes, displaying two depressed semicircles within the frequency range of 1 mHz–100 kHz. Generally, the first semicircle at high frequency can be attributed to Li+ migration through the SEI film covering the electrodes, whereas the other semicircle (middle-frequency) is assigned to charge-transfer through the electrode/electrolyte interface. Finally, the low-frequency slant line mainly originates from the solid-state diffusion of Li ion through the electrodes [60–62]. An equivalent circuit model (see Fig. 7(a)) including R S (resistance of the electrolyte, electrode, and separator), R SEI (resistances of SEI film), R CT (charge-transfer resistance), CPE1 (the constant phase elements for the capacitances of SEI film), and CPE2 (the constant phase elements for the capacitances of the double layer), was used for fitting the EIS spectra. The Z-view software package was employed and the model parameters were determined for each electrode as tabulated in Table 3 . The deviations between the experimentally recorded EIS spectra and the model predictions was less than 10% for all the samples within the entire frequency range (i.e. 100 kHz to 5 Hz). It can be seen from Table 3 that C3 electrode exhibits the lowest R SEI value among the NGL electrodes. This can be attributed to the fact that C3 electrode possesses the lowest oxidation level as compared to the other two samples. The R CT values of the NGL electrode were measured within the range of 6.1–8.5 Ω for all the as-prepared anode materials. The equivalent series resistance, R ES (= R S + R SEI + R CT), was also assessed to determine the overall internal resistance of the cells [63]. The R ES value was found to have the following sequence among the NGL electrodes: C3 (30.1 Ω) < C2 (32.3 Ω) < C1 (38.3 Ω). Therefore, the charge transfer resistance (R CT) is the dominant source of overpotential for the NGL anode materials where the C3 electrode exhibits the lowest internal resistance among the N-doped graphite-like crystals.

To further analyze the high-frequency resistance observed for the NGL electrodes, the four-probe conductivity technique was adopted to measure the σ avg values of the electrode sheets. The σ avg values of G, C1, C2, and C3 electrodes were 1.43, 2.03, 2.43, and 2.77 S cm−1, respectively. The σ avg value of C3 electrode was found to have an increase up to 93.7%, as compared to that of G one. This result demonstrates that N-doping into graphite-like structure substantially improves the electrical conductivity since the presence of graphitic N (i.e., n-type doping) facilitates the charge carrier mobility within the carbon anodes, resulting in an improved σ avg value [44–46].

As shown in Fig. 7(a), the Nyquist plot displays an inclined line at ∼45° to Re Z′ axis, resulted from the solid-state diffusion process of Li ions in the lattice [64–66]. Meanwhile, Warburg impedance (Z D) in the electrodes can be formulated as Z D = k W ω −1/2 (1 − j), based on semi-infinite diffusion model [67]. Here, k W represents the Warburg coefficient and ω (= 2πƒ) is the angular frequency (f). Fig. 7(b) presents the “Randles plot”, Re Z’ versus ω −1/2, for the NGL electrodes. As shown in Fig. 7(b), two sets of linear plots can be identified for each electrode, implying the diffusion of Li ions in heterogeneous structure including C- and N-active sites. Thus, the diffusion coefficient of Li ions (D Li), determined from the k w value, were averaged and as listed in Table 2. The D Li value for C3 electrode was 1.69 × 10−9 cm2 s−1, higher other NGL and G anode materials. The improved Li diffusion coefficient for the C3 electrode is due to higher porosity (as verified via BET surface area measurement) as well as increased pyrrolic N sites within NGL lattices, as confirmed by N2-physisorption and XPS analyses. The magnitude of D Li achieved for the NGL electrodes in this work is significantly higher than the values reported for the conventional graphite electrodes ranged from 1.12 × 10−10 to 6.51 × 10−11 cm2 s−1 [68]. Therefore, the NGL electrode materials increase the D Li value by 15–26 times. This finding reveals that the ionic diffusion resistance is significantly alleviated. This can be attributed to two possible reasons: (i) short diffusion path and (ii) more surface active sites for Li-storage. The pyridinic N and pyrrolic N formed at the edges and vacancy sites of the NGL electrode materials promote the transport of Li ions through the lattice structure via shortening the Li ion diffusion pathway; thus, significantly enhance the rate capability [50,69]. Moreover, the expanding of interlayer distance, due to the N dopants, tends to alleviate the diffusion resistance, facilitating high rate capability [53]. The result is well consistent with the analyses of high C-rate performance (capacity retention: 82.4% at 5C) and charge/discharge cycling (at 1C for 200 cycles). When Li ions diffuse to reach the active sites, the sites provide high binding energy with Li ions, consequently, increase the Li storage capacity [26].

Finally, the performance of NGL anode materials was evaluated in real time within a full pouch cell. The pouch cell, including the NCM cathode and NGL anode (e.g. C3), was assembled and the discharge capacity as a function of cycle number at different rates was assessed (see Fig. 8 (a)). The pouch cells demonstrated rate capability at 0.1–5C, where the capacity retention at 5C–0.1C was ∼42.%. As compared to high rate capability of NGL half-cell (i.e., capacity retention: 79.2% at 5C), higher capacity decay observed for the full pouch cell is due to inefficient transport of Li ions and the charge transfer in NCM cathode that becomes the rate-limiting step. The NCM cathode materials should be nanosized (i.e. thinner thickness of cathode) or surface-modified to improve the high rate performance. Fig. 8(b) exhibits the cyclic stability test of the pouch cell charged and discharged at 1C. The inset of Fig. 8(b) shows the pictures of NGL electrode sheet (on Cu foil) and the pouch cell. As illustrated in Fig. 8(b), the discharge capacity remains stable with only 4.7% decay upon 100 cycles. The stabilized coulombic efficiency of the pouch cell was maintained >99.1% during the entire cycling. Accordingly, we believe that the nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots anode material paves the way for developing next-generation LIBs.

The NGL materials were synthesized via thermal pyrolysis of N-functionalized GQDs in hydrogen atmosphere at 1000 °C, and were served as the anode for Li-ion batteries. The NGL anode demonstrated reversible capacity of 530 mAh g−1 at 0.1C, very high initial coulombic efficiency, superior capacity retention at 5C, and excellent cyclic stability with high coulombic efficiency during entire cycling. The improved performance was majorly due to (i) doped N atoms enhance the reactivity and electrical conductivity of the NGL anodes due to larger electronegativity of N atoms and (ii) the introduction of N atoms creates more edges within the electrode nanostructure as well as open and flexible vacancy defects for efficient Li-storage. The Ragone plots revealed that the specific energy of NGL anode reaches to ca. 840 Wh kg−1 at 4200 W kg−1. The D Li value of NGL anode, as analyzed by Randles plot, was 1.69 × 10−9 cm2 s−1 (15–26 times higher than the graphite anodes). The high rate cyclability and cyclic stability of the NGL anodes were also confirmed in-situ via cycling a full pouch cell assembled with the NCM cathode and NGL anode. The NGL electrode generates an N-doped framework promoting ionic mobility within the electrode structure with superior Li storage and electronic conductivity. Therefore, the robust performance of NGL anode materials enables high rate cyclability for next-generation Li-ion batteries operating stably at ultra-high C rates.

Siyong Gu: Data curation, Visualization, Investigation. Tommiejean Christensen: Data curation, Visualization, Investigation. Chien-Te Hsieh: Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Bikash Chandra Mallick: Visualization. Yasser Ashraf Gandomi: Writing - review & editing, Validation. Jianlin Li: Supervision, Writing - review & editing, Methodology. Jeng-Kuei Chang: Supervision, Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation.
